abstract:
The lives of international organizations (IOs) frequently extend far beyond their initial founding. Yet, how do these institutions adapt to shifting domestic politics, global geopolitical realignments, and their own internal institutional pressures? This special issue introduces a life-cycle framework to explore the fluid nature of the international cooperation that underpins the global system. While firm theory, organizational sociology, and agency theory have long utilized life-cycle perspectives, international relations scholarship has yet to fully integrate these dynamic frameworks.

A life-cycle approach shifts the focus in two critical ways: first, it establishes the IO itself as the primary unit of analysis, and second, it tracks the non-linear processes inherent in organizational existence—including stages such as false starts, consolidation, inertia, revitalization, and succession. By documenting these phases, we are reminded that international cooperation has always been characterized by periods of both flourishing and faltering. Understanding the mechanisms driving these transitions is essential for assessing the overall resilience of the international order. 

The articles in this issue examine the durability of IOs during economic crises; the proactive strategies secretariats use to legitimize their authority; the role of leader rhetoric in shaping institutional vitality; the strategic trade-offs between reforming an existing body versus creating a successor; and the systemic and domestic consequences of member-state withdrawal. Together, these contributions offer a more nuanced, historically grounded perspective on the vitality of global governance.
